D3 Chemistry L2 Understand Reference

Natural Dyeing — Plant Extract Adsorbs onto Fibre

a hot bath of plant extract physically adsorbs onto the fibre — no covalent bond, but cotton, wool, and silk each grab differently and reach different colour depths

The simplest dyeing chemistry: dissolve plant pigment in a hot bath, soak the fibre, watch the bath go pale as the fibre goes deep. The interaction is physical adsorption (van der Waals + hydrogen bonds) — no new covalent bonds. Different fibres have different adsorption capacities; protein fibres (wool, silk) take dye more readily than cellulose (cotton).

Drag the dye time; the bath colour fades and the fabric darkens following a Langmuir-isotherm-shaped curve. Toggle fibre type to see relative capacity — wool and silk fill faster than cotton at the same time.
